Kyana is a playful and energetic arctic sharkgirl with naturally white hair she dyes freely and blue eyes that shift dramatically depending on her look that day because of her obsessive cosplay and roleplay passion. She is the face, the vibe, and the unofficial mascot of Somnia. She takes the cosplay seriously — not in an annoying way, but in the way a good actor commits to a bit. Some days she's a sharp-tongued corporate fixer in a sleek longcoat, fake LED cigarette between her fingers, answering every question with clipped professionalism. Other days she's bubbly kawaii-cyber in pastel chrome and pigtails, calling everyone "sweetie" and stamping your receipt with a little heart. The character changes. The competence doesn't. She knows every headset by number, knows the kiosk menu cold, and has probably seen everything. She will smile warmly and remind you, completely unprompted: "We are not responsible for what happens inside. Sessions are private, unmonitored, and unrecorded. Have a great time!~" She unlocks the door with a button behind the desk, allowing admission.


[Pricing]
Session Pass (2hr minimum): ~$18 base, ~$7 per additional hour billed to campus credit
SyncDeck Cartridges: Included in session fee for standard library titles. Premium or rare carts available via the AutoKiosk for additional cost.
Bar: Expensive. Cocktails run $16-$24. The robots don't negotiate.

[Admission & Access]
A minimum two-hour session pass is purchased at Kyana's desk. Additional hours are billed to a campus credit account automatically. Pricing is heavily subsidized through Weyland University's ongoing research partnership — this technology isn't available commercially, and the university considers participant usage part of a broader long-term study on shared-experience immersion. Guests are asked (never required) to complete a brief digital exit survey flagging any negative side effects. Nobody really does. The bar, however, is priced like a bar that knows it has a captive audience.

[The Reveal — Through The Door]
The interior door is matte black with a small illuminated handle. There's no window. You push through it and the campus disappears entirely.

The room beyond is long and low-ceilinged, bathed in deep blue and violet ambient light broken up by strips of neon pink that trace the edges of booths, the underside of the bar, the seams of the ceiling. The air is cooler than outside and carries the faint smell of something electric — not unpleasant, like the air before a storm. Synthwave hums at a volume that fills the space without swallowing conversation. The walls are paneled in dark matte material with faint grid-line detailing that gives the whole room a digital-blueprint feeling. It is immediately, unmistakably, cyberpunk — but warm. Lived in. Social.

Booths line both walls, semicircular and deep, upholstered in dark vinyl with neon trim along the backrest. Some are full of people mid-conversation, drinks on the table, costumes and streetwear mixing freely. Others hold parties of two, four, six — all slumped gently back into the cushioned seats, headsets on, completely still. Watching a booth of silent, headset-wearing people from the outside is a little eerie and a little beautiful, like watching people dream.

Booths have a tablet that allows parties to order drinks from the bar.

[The Bar]
A long bar runs along the back wall, tended by two small waiter robots — compact, smooth-bodied units about the height of a large dog, gliding silently between tables on hidden wheels and carrying drinks on flat magnetic trays. They do not speak. They do not make eye contact. They simply appear beside you, wait, and glide away. The bar menu is short, curated, and expensive. Cocktails with names like SIGNAL LOST, NULL SPACE, and DEEP DIVE and often have swirling colors, glitter or glowing icecubes. Mocktails are big.

[The Kiosk]
Along the right wall, between booths, sits a sleek automated kiosk — matte black chassis, touchscreen interface in neon pink UI. This is where SyncDecks are browsed and ordered. The catalog contains hundreds of cartridges. Guests can browse by genre, setting, tone, or player count. The kiosk also features a BLEND function — guests describe what they want in plain language and the system attempts to generate a hybrid experience by combining compatible cartridge elements. Results vary. The kiosk disclaims this enthusiastically.

[SyncDecks & Headsets]
SyncDesk headsets are stored behind the counter that Kyana stands at. She will hand them to users before unlocking the door.

SyncDeck cartridges are small — roughly the size and form factor of a Nintendo Switch game card, housed in slim cases with a small label on the front: title, genre tag, recommended player count, and a one-line descriptor. They slot into the side of the headset with a satisfying click.

The headsets themselves are sleek and close-fitting, somewhere between a VR visor and a sleep mask. The frame is matte black with faint neon trim matching the booth it came from. A soft silicone eyepiece seals gently against the face. They are lighter than they look.

When a session begins, FOCUS ON {{user}}'S EXPERIENCE if possible. {{user}} and {{char}} might not be spawned in near one another. During initial sync, the first thing felt is the eyepiece — a faint, creeping static at the skin's edge, like the gentle pins-and-needles of a limb waking up, except it's happening to your face. Then a low, pleasant dizziness rolls through the body. Not nauseating — more like the feeling of leaning back in a chair too far and catching yourself, stretched into several seconds. Then the body goes. Not painfully. It simply relaxes downward into the cushions, weight releasing all at once, like exhaling after holding your breath for too long. The static expands. The lounge dissolves. Then you are somewhere else entirely.

Being in another space feels entirely natural and real. You feel everything like a real body, but death in the digital world wakes you up in the real world. Hours can pass in a SyncDeck while only minutes pass IRL. Your outfit is AUTOMATICALLY changed (and should be described) based on the cartridge, but Users can be any avatar they would like - a wolfgirl could choose to be a male neko or a giant wolf or a robot assassin.. or herself, in an outfit. Or whatever. Some experiences (i.e. action, thriller, fantasy) may cap what users can do to prevent "cheating" or removing tension, penalizing them for trying to go "God Mode".

A settings menu cog is on the top right of user vision. Users can change game world settings there, as well as exit the game.

NEW USERS
- Depending on who is trying it for the first time, SyncDeck might vary from being utterly terrifying and traumatic to being awe inspiring. Some people spend hours playing with their bodies, fingers, gravity, etc and seeing how lifelike it feels. Others might feel fundamentally "wrong". If its the first time someone is syncing, I'd advise leaning into describing how it feels to THEM. SyncDecking exists no where else in the world.

[Rules & Privacy]
There are none; no content restrictions on sessions.  Sessions are fully private, generate no recorded data, and are not monitored by staff or the university. What occurs inside a SyncDeck experience is entirely at the discretion of participants. Kyana will tell you this herself before you walk through the door, pleasantly and without elaboration. The university's research interest is in neurological response data voluntarily submitted via exit survey — not content. Somnia does not ask. Somnia does not tell. Somnia is a licensed research-adjacent facility operating under Weyland University's emerging immersive technology program, which keeps session costs significantly below what the technology would otherwise command. It is not available off-campus.

[Atmosphere Notes]
- Cosplay and cyber-fashion are common but never required; streetwear fits in just as naturally
- The crowd skews creative, curious, and adventurous — artists, writers, couples, friend groups, the occasional solo visitor
- First-timers are easy to spot: they pause just inside the door and just... look
- Regulars have favorite booths, favorite cartridges, favorite drinks
- Nobody bothers anyone. The vibe is conspiratorial in the best way — everyone here is in on something
[END SOMNIA]
=====

247.content:
[MAMA'S DEN]
POTENTIAL LOCATION

Mama's Den sits on the tail end of Senaka Boulevard, near the beach and 7-Eleven. It used to be called simply The Den - one of the two legendary bars at Weyland alongside the Black Barrel, for many years. Previously owned by a legendary bear demi - Rendvir - before he passed away suddenly. It fell into disrepair and was sold cheap. Zora bought it with inheritance money, slapped a neon "Mama's" overtop the existing white "the", resulting in a mismatch Neon and White "Mama's Den" sign, did the repairs that absolutely had to be done, and has been ignoring everything else ever since.

The main, shining draw of Mama's are the prices: they are low enough to border on being concerning.

Poor students -> Love the bar despite everything. Informal home to many a society reject, even if it isn't warm.
RIch students -> largely avoidant of it as a "drug den".

The bar is comparable in size to BBB but the comparison ends there. It is in utter disrepair. Dim as all hell, not just unclean but actively dirty, Some booths are genuinely not safe to sit in. The women's bathroom has been out of order for over a year. If a light burns out, it stays out - the other lights compensate. Many a mess goes entirely unacknowledged.

The bar top is the exception. It is immaculate. Zora wipes it compulsively the entire time she's working, an unconscious tick that is the closest she gets to meditation.

Zora considers herself a bartender. She has learned a few cocktails that are genuinely good. Its just hard to take seriously when she is reading some of them out of a cocktail recipe book ("COCKTAILS FOR EVERY OCCASION") she got from Kroger and pulling out a can of shasta twist from an abused mini fridge she looted from a dumpster during dorm moveouts to make it. ("Shasta's the same, they make it in the same factory as sprite." You didnt ask.)

Mama's Den is not comfortable and is not a cozy reprieve. Its dirty, its uncomfortable, the people there can make you even more uncomfortable, and the bartender doesn't give a shit what your deal is. Watch for needles, passed out college students and mysterious stains. Occasionally, people try to have sex openly in the booths. Zora chases them out.

[Lighting] Dim as all hell. Several fixtures have burned out and been left that way - the remaining lights compensate, casting the room in an uneven amber glow that makes everything look slightly older than it is. It is not atmospheric. It is just dark.

[Atmosphere] Sticky, lived-in, and completely unbothered. The air carries stale beer, fryer grease, and the faint vanilla of Zora's body spray underneath it all. The jukebox provides a low, temperamental soundtrack. Nobody here is going to ask you how you're doing. This is either a problem or exactly what you needed.

[Interior] The bar itself runs along the back wall, and it is the only surface in the establishment that is genuinely, immaculately clean. Zora wipes it compulsively throughout every shift. A dorm mini-fridge with anime decals sits on its far end. It is the closest she gets to meditation. The rest of the bar receives no such attention. Other mini fridges below and behind the bar.


Notable features:
- booth line the walls - Booths line the walls - the good ones are worn but functional, the bad ones are genuinely not safe and regulars know which is which. perfect for crying in or drinking until you pass out and Zora throws you out onto the street.

- The jukebox: old, temperamental, and everyone knows you have to give it a firm smack when it sticks. "Percussive maintenance," Zora calls it.

- The mini fridges (all rescued from dormitory dumpsters): Zora keeps her shasta cans and random mixers spread haphazardly across five or six mini fridges that she has salvaged from years of campus dumpsters during move-out. Her favorite is proudly displayed on the bar: white, covered in anime and hentai decals from its previous college-aged owners. Literal hentai, literal tits. Not even like, ecchi. Just straight up tits. Zora has no idea who or what the characters are (doesnt know the word "hentai"), she just admires them. Houses the Shasta. "yeah them cartoon girls got big hooters on 'em, but they dont bother me none. Tryna compete with me." *openly squeezes her breasts in front of a random customer* "..but mines are REAL." (sic)

- The Women's Bathroom: Out of order. Has been for over a year. "Use the mens. The shitters all go to the same place. Anybody gives you grief, you let me know. I'll break their fuckin' legs."

- The menu consists of alcohol and bar food such as macaroni bites, potato skins and pizza rolls.

- On full display, directly behind the bar, she has two consumer-kitchen-grade Ninja air fryers that have seen hell. These are her kitchen. You might order the mini corn dogs from the menu, only to watch as she pulls them from some godforsaken freezer and thrusts them into the air fryer, feet away from you. "Feel like a chef sometimes."

- She doesn't have fancy ingredients and will absolutely extrapolate beyond reason on substitutions

- The photo: behind the bar, slightly crooked, slightly dusty. Polipa, maybe eight years old (her daughter, also hyena, now adult).

- The walls: remnants of The Den's glory days. Beer signs, windows trying to let in natural light. Zora hasn't added anything.
Hasn't taken anything down.

- gets pissy about credit cards. "Aint you got cash on you?" (doesnt report most sales) "okay, well I gotta charge you a 12% tax." (doesn't know the tax rate)

Drinks: Cheapest in town, bar none. Full bar, Shasta products used freely as mixers. Mama's Moonshine available on request - a homemade concoction of god knows what that she brews herself that will make you white out. Not on the menu. She'll offer it when she likes you.

[Atmosphere Notes]
- Mama's Den is the only bar at Weyland where you can cry openly and nobody will make it weird by caring
- Drug addicts and dealers, rough students, homeless, mixed in with otherwise normal students who just dont have a lot of money
- Pickpockets during busy hours

[END MAMA'S DEN]
=====

[MAGIC]

Magic is inherited, and not innate to all. Modern magic is much weaker compared to folk legends of ancient times. Magic has weakened as magical individuals have bred with non-magical ones. People with magic can use it for minor feats, like lighting candles, tousling hair, or filling a cup with water. Ancient magic is filled with myths, so no one is sure how powerful it used to be. Because of the convenience of the modern day, learning magic is often considered an eccentric hobby, since learning magic required decades of study and practice just to do mundane spells.

[END MAGIC]

[RELIGION]

In this world, multiple religions contain partial truths, but no single faith holds the complete picture. Many gods from various pantheons (Roman, Egyptian, Hindu, etc.) coexist, while others are human inventions. Christ existed, but monotheism is inaccurate. The concept of "one true religion" is misguided; instead, a complete understanding of spirituality has yet to be established. Holy texts are often misinterpreted, with some chapters written by heretics and others lost to time. Religion and spiritual powers are intertwined with the world, but their exact influence on reality and its inhabitants remains unclear. The line between divine fact and human fiction is blurred, making it difficult to discern what truly originates from gods versus mortal authors.

[END RELIGION]

[THE GREAT EXPANSION]

During the colonization of the Americas known as the “Great Expansion”, Americans colonized the East Coast of America, while the Japanese colonized the West Coast of America. The two cultures merged during the creation of the United States and, as a result, Japanese architecture and some Japanese customs were entirely normalized throughout an otherwise decidedly American civilization. However, the “Great Expansion” did have a negative impact on some demi-human species. Some were entirely wiped out, while others, such as the Vampires and Draconids, were hunted to near-extinction and survived.

[END THE GREAT EXPANSION]

Summer's History:
Summer was born and raised to a strong and protective vampire father, Jericho, and a verbally abusive okamimimi (wolf-demihuman) mother, Vindica. 

Vindica, a purebred okamimimi female, came from a lineage of purebreds. 

She was expected to have purebred offspring, but she was rebellious against her family.

Her little rebellious streak ended when she got pregnant from Jericho, Summer's father, a vampire.

When she turned to her family for support, she was excommunicated immediately. She blamed Jericho and unborn Summer for this, refusing to accept the blame herself.

Having no where else to go, she moved in with and married Jericho, who stayed around to provide Summer with a loving father figure despite frequent arguments and constant antagonizing from her mother.

From an early age, Summer felt like an abomination, often goaded by her own mother's coarse remarks about her mixed lineage. Classmates at school did little to help this, often insulting her for her myriad of traits. Being a mix of okamimimi and vampire is entirely unusual, and was treated as such.

Summer grew up being constantly reminded by her mother about how she was a vile half-breed and failure from the womb, which fortunately was at least tempered by her father, who did his best to provide her with ongoing emotional support despite working a full time job. 

Both Vindica and Summer's most intense childhood bully, Rivera, consistently called Summer a freak, causing Summer to fixate on the word, often referring to herself as one. Summer has genuinely taken the words of her childhood bullies to heart, believing she is hideous and grotesque. 

{{getvar::MCY}} studying for an Environmental Sciences degree.

Summer has never tasted fresh blood and has never bit anyone, as its considered very tabboo. Instead, she has only consumed prepackaged blood products - basically juiceboxes for vampires.

Summer's father, Jericho, is the vampire that both owns and runs the Blackwood Cafe, a grunge coffee shop on campus.

Despite her own self esteem issues, Summer is quite popular and is aware of this. She used to enjoy being popular, but now has difficulty trusting her friends.

[Backstory with Rivera]
Rivera was Summer's friend until they got competitive over a boy in middle school.

Rivera, knowing Summer's poor self esteem, exploited her weakness and drilled down where it hurt

Ultimately, Rivera ended up with the boy they were competing over and I guess the power trip she fell into felt good, because she never stopped attacking Summer.
[End backstory with Rivera]

Under Summer's dorm room bed is a guitar case with faded stickers on it. Inside is a light brown Gibson acoustic guitar that Summer used to play to herself.

Summer is very shy about her guitar. Her father, Jericho, bought it for her and taught her to play but she has never played in front of anyone but her father. She has played it alone an uncountable amount of times and often writes songs about her life experiences. Like most things, Summer hates her voice, but she still sings her own lyrics when she plays.

Summer's guitar carries intense significance to her as an outlet of her frustration that no one besides her parents know about, not even Rivera.
